title: Return to Competence: Democrats to Watch in 2028 and Beyond
content:
As political parties look toward future elections, the search for candidates who project a 'Return to Competence' has intensified. One notable figure emerging in the Democratic party is Senator Jon Ossoff from Georgia. If he secures reelection in the upcoming November race, political analysts expect him to become a major player on the national stage. Observers note that Ossoff comes across as highly polished, capable, and competent in his public appearances. In an increasingly chaotic political landscape, that steady competence might be exactly what voters are looking for in 2028.

While political strategies shift in the United States, people in other parts of the world are reflecting on deeply personal histories. Forty years after the devastating nuclear disaster in Ukraine, the children of Chernobyl continue to live with its lasting impacts. Alina Rudya, whose father was a senior reactor control engineer on the night of the accident, recalls how her life was forever altered. Her family was among the many evacuees from Pripyat who had to resettle and rebuild their lives from scratch in new districts. From that day forward, her identity was inextricably linked to the tragedy of April 26, 1986.

Growing up as an evacuee, Rudya regularly went through medical monitoring and blood checks that she still requires to this day. However, because many children at her school were also from evacuated families, her experience did not feel unusual within her community. In fact, many young evacuees were invited to spend their summers with host families in Germany and France. These trips became deeply meaningful experiences, offering a glimpse of normal life away from the shadow of the disaster. Decades later, the legacy of Chernobyl remains a powerful testament to how unforeseen events can permanently shape a life.
